attributes.stem: A 72-year-old man with a history of coronary artery disease presents with a one-week history of intermittent blurring of vision in his right eye. He describes the loss of vision as 'dimming' of the vision that lasts for a few minutes and then resolves spontaneously. The blurring of vision does not seem to be associated with a particular activity, happening both at rest and during physical activity alike. There is no pain associated with the blurring of vision. This only lasts a few minutes and then his vision returns to normal. Patient does not report any associated halos, flashes or floaters.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following physical signs is most likely to be present?
options: Increased intraocular pressure in the right eye
Tenderness over the right temporal artery
attributes.explanation:
This patient is suffering from a monocular transient visual loss (MTVL). The differential diagnosis of monocular transient visual loss is further divided into painful and painless causes.

The most important aetiology of a painless MTVL in an older patient with cardiovascular risk factors is symptomatic carotid artery disease. An unstable atherosclerotic plaque in the left carotid arteries (mainly CCA and ICA) could embolise distally resulting in Amaurosis Fugax or MTVL. Clinically, it is common for an ipsilateral carotid bruit to be auscultated in the setting of a haemodynamically significant stenosis.

Increased intraocular pressure from intermittent angle closure glaucoma may present in this age group however, MTVL would be associated with blurry vision, halos, and ipsilateral pain or headache. If the patient presented during the attack, redness, corneal oedema and a mid-dilated pupil might be seen. However if the patient presented at other times, blurry vision and pain would not be present. Instead, gonioscopy would show synechial angle closure or glaucoma. Flecks or localised infarcted whitish areas may be present on the anterior lens capsule and cupping of the disc would be seen.

Right-sided ptosis and miosis are part of Horner's syndrome. Horner's syndrome is not associated with decreased or loss of vision. Painful Horner's syndrome in a patient with this demographic should alert the examiner to a possibility of a dissecting carotid artery which is a life-threatening condition. This patient does not have pain and the time frame of the presentation makes this diagnosis less likely. Horner’s syndrome may be the result by many aetiologies affecting the sympathetic pathway that innervates the dilator pupillae muscle. Examples of conditions that cause Horner’s syndrome also include lesions affecting the hypothalamus such as a stroke or demyelination, pancoast tumors, and cluster headaches.

MTV associated with tenderness of the scalp raises the possibility of temporal arteritis commonly associated with giant cell arteritis. This condition is more common however in women and would be associated with other constitutional signs such as low-grade fever and generalised muscle pain and weakness. This patient is not complaining of any pain.
learningPoints: Painless intermittent MTVL usually indicated carotid artery atherosclerosis
Atherosclerosis of the carotid artery causes narrowing of the artery which manifests as a carotid bruit on examination
references: Amaurosis Fugax (Transient Vision Loss) - EyeWiki [Internet]. eyewiki.org. [cited 2022 Jul 24]. Available from: https://eyewiki.org/Amaurosis_Fugax_(Transient_Vision_Loss)
